title: The Backend — The Brain of TesTly
subtitle: The server that runs everything behind the scenes
intro: TesTly is a website and phone app where people in Egypt can find a medical lab, book a test, pay, and get their results. But the website and the app are just the screens you see. The backend is the brain that does the real work behind them.
bullets: It listens to the web app and the phone app and answers their requests.
It checks the rules (who is allowed to do what) and keeps data safe.
It talks to the database, where all the information is stored.
Same brain serves both the website and the phone app.

title: What Does the Backend Actually Do?
intro: Think of it like a waiter between you and the kitchen.

steps.title: 1. It receives a request
steps.detail: When you tap 'Book this test' on the app, a message is sent to the backend asking it to do something.

steps.title: 2. It checks the rules
steps.detail: Are you logged in? Are you allowed to do this? Is the lab real and open? It refuses anything that breaks the rules.

steps.title: 3. It talks to the database
steps.detail: The database is the big organised storage of all data (users, labs, bookings, results). The backend reads from it or saves new information into it.

steps.title: 4. It sends back an answer
steps.detail: It replies to the app with a clear result, like 'Booking confirmed' or 'That time is already taken'.

title: Where the Backend Sits in the System
imageCaption: The web app and phone app both talk to one backend, which talks to the database and a few helper services.
bullets: Two front doors (website + phone app), one shared brain.
The backend is the only thing allowed to touch the database directly.
Helper services (file storage, push notifications, AI) hang off the side.

title: Tech Stack & Why We Chose It
intro: These are the main building blocks of the backend, and the reason for each.

cards.name: NestJS
cards.what: A toolkit for building the server in an organised, tidy way, splitting the code into neat sections called modules.
cards.why: It keeps a big project from turning into spaghetti, with clear places to add login checks, rules, and features.

cards.name: TypeScript
cards.what: JavaScript with type-checking, meaning it warns you when you use data the wrong way.
cards.why: It catches many mistakes while we write code, before users ever see a bug.

cards.name: PostgreSQL (Neon)
cards.what: The actual storage where all data lives. Neon is a cloud version that runs online without us managing a server.
cards.why: It is reliable, well-tested, and great at keeping money and medical data correct and consistent.

cards.name: Prisma
cards.what: A translator that lets our code talk to the database in plain, safe commands instead of raw database language.
cards.why: It makes database code easier and safer to write, and blocks a whole class of hacking attacks.

cards.name: JWT + Passport
cards.what: JWT is a signed digital ticket that proves who you are. Passport is the helper that checks that ticket on every request.
cards.why: It lets the server remember you are logged in without storing a session for every user, so it scales easily.

cards.name: bcrypt
cards.what: A tool that scrambles passwords into unreadable text before saving them.
cards.why: If the database were ever stolen, the real passwords still cannot be read.

title: Tech Stack & Why (continued)
intro: The helper services that handle files, alerts, documentation, and safety.

cards.name: AWS S3
cards.what: An online storage locker for big files, used to keep the lab result PDF documents.
cards.why: PDFs are too big to put in a normal database, and S3 stores them cheaply and safely at any scale.

cards.name: Firebase Cloud Messaging
cards.what: Google's service for sending pop-up alerts to phones, like 'Your result is ready'.
cards.why: It is the standard, free, reliable way to reach both iPhone and Android phones.

cards.name: Swagger
cards.what: Auto-generated, browsable documentation listing every command the backend understands.
cards.why: It lets the team test and understand the backend without guessing, saving lots of time.

cards.name: class-validator
cards.what: A tool that checks incoming data is the right shape (a real email, a price that is a number, etc.).
cards.why: It stops bad or broken data at the front door before it can cause errors deeper in the system.

title: Inside the Backend: The Modules
imageCaption: The backend is split into clear sections, each handling one job.
bullets: Bookings handles ordering and paying for tests.
Results handles the result PDFs and the health-trend data.
Each section is small and focused, so it is easy to understand and fix.

title: How Login & User Roles Work
intro: How the backend knows who you are and what you are allowed to do.
imageCaption: Login gives you two tickets, and guards check them on every request.

steps.title: 1. You log in
steps.detail: You send your email and password. The backend uses bcrypt to check the password matches the scrambled one it stored.

steps.title: 2. You get two tickets
steps.detail: An access token (a short-lived ticket valid for 8 hours that proves who you are) and a refresh token (a long-lived ticket used to get a new access token later).

steps.title: 3. Every request shows the ticket
steps.detail: The website carries the ticket in a secure cookie; the phone app carries it in the request header. One backend understands both.

steps.title: 4. Guards check your role
steps.detail: There are three roles: Patient, Lab Staff, and Admin. 'Role guards' make sure a patient can't open lab-only pages, and a not-yet-approved lab can't sell tests.

title: How Booking + Payment Works
intro: What happens from picking a time slot to paying for the test.
imageCaption: The patient books a slot, the backend locks it in safely, then payment and confirmation follow.

steps.title: 1. See free time slots
steps.detail: The app asks the backend which appointment times are still open at the chosen lab, and shows them to the patient.

steps.title: 2. Create the booking
steps.detail: The backend checks the rules (the lab is open, the test exists, the slot is free) and works out the price, including any home-visit fee.

steps.title: 3. Lock the slot safely
steps.detail: It saves the booking in one safe all-or-nothing step so the same time slot can never be grabbed by two people at once.

steps.title: 4. Pay, then confirm
steps.detail: The patient pays online or with cash. The lab then confirms the booking, and the patient gets a notification. An online booking can only be confirmed after it is paid.

title: How Lab Results Get Delivered
intro: How a test result travels from the lab to the patient, safely.
imageCaption: The lab uploads the PDF and the numbers, then the patient is notified and can download it.

steps.title: 1. Lab uploads the official PDF
steps.detail: The backend double-checks the file really is a PDF (not just named like one) and keeps it under 10 MB, then stores it in S3.

steps.title: 2. Lab adds the numbers
steps.detail: The lab also enters the actual readings (like glucose level). These get cleaned up into a standard form for health trends.

steps.title: 3. Mark as delivered
steps.detail: The lab marks the result delivered. The booking is now complete and the patient gets a 'Result Ready' notification.

steps.title: 4. Patient downloads it
steps.detail: Only the right patient, lab, or admin can open the file. The backend checks this every time and never shares the raw storage link.

title: How the Data Is Organised
imageCaption: Every user, lab, booking, and result is a table, linked together. A Booking sits at the center.
bullets: A booking connects a patient, a lab, and a test together.
Each booking can have a result file and structured readings.
Storing data in linked tables keeps it tidy and prevents contradictions.

title: Clever Bit 1: Comparing Results Across Different Labs
intro: Different labs write the same test in different names and different units. So a patient who switches labs can't easily see if their health is improving or getting worse. We fix that by converting everything to one common form.

steps.title: Match the name
steps.detail: 'Fasting glucose', 'FBS', and 'Fasting Blood Sugar' all mean the same thing. The backend uses a dictionary to recognise them as one marker.

steps.title: Convert the unit
steps.detail: One lab reports glucose as 5.4 mmol/L, another as 97 mg/dL. The backend converts both to the same unit using fixed medical formulas (for glucose, multiply by 18).

steps.title: Flag anything unsure
steps.detail: If a unit isn't recognised, it is marked 'not comparable' instead of guessing, so a wrong number never goes on the patient's health chart.

why: It lets a patient see one honest health trend over time, even when their tests came from several different labs.

title: Clever Bit 2: Two People Can't Grab the Same Slot
intro: Imagine two patients tap 'Book' on the exact same 10:00 AM slot at the exact same moment. Without protection, both could be booked and the lab is double-booked. We make that impossible.

steps.title: A database rule: one slot, one booking
steps.detail: The database is told that each time slot can be linked to only one active booking. It will physically refuse a second one.

steps.title: All-or-nothing save (a transaction)
steps.detail: The booking is saved as a single bundled step. If anything goes wrong, the whole thing is undone, never half-saved.

steps.title: Loser gets a friendly message
steps.detail: Whoever's request arrives second is cleanly rejected with 'That slot is already booked', instead of a crash or a double-booking.

why: It guarantees the schedule is always correct, even when many people book at the very same second.

title: Clever Bit 3: Staying Logged In, Safely
intro: We want users to stay logged in for a long time without re-typing their password constantly. But a long-lived login ticket is dangerous if stolen. The trick is to rotate the ticket.

steps.title: Short ticket + long ticket
steps.detail: The access ticket expires fast (8 hours). The refresh ticket lasts longer (30 days) and is used quietly to get a fresh access ticket.

steps.title: Rotate on every use
steps.detail: Each time the refresh ticket is used, the old one is thrown away and a brand-new one is issued. The old one stops working immediately.

steps.title: Stored scrambled
steps.detail: Only a scrambled fingerprint of the refresh ticket is saved, never the real one, so a stolen database is useless to an attacker.

why: If a refresh ticket is ever stolen, it works at most once before it is rotated out, sharply limiting the damage.

title: Code: How Distance Is Calculated
intro: Every lab has a latitude and longitude stored in the database. The backend calculates the straight-line distance to the user using the haversine formula — a standard formula that works on a curved Earth, not a flat map.
note: **R = 6371** is the mean radius of the Earth in kilometres. The formula converts the lat/lng difference into radians, computes an intermediate value `a` (a squared half-chord length on the sphere), then uses `atan2` to convert it to an arc length. The result is multiplied by R to get kilometres. The same formula runs identically in TypeScript (frontend) and Dart (mobile app) — so every client shows the same distance for the same lab.

title: How Smart Search Works: 5 Steps to the Right Result
intro: When a user types 'HBA1', 'glucos', or 'فحص السكر', the search still finds the right test. That does not happen by accident — the query runs through a 5-step pipeline combining synonym expansion, SQL fuzzy matching, and typo correction.

steps.title: Step 1 — Normalize
steps.detail: The query is lowercased and split into tokens. 'Fasting Blood Sugar Test' becomes ['fasting', 'blood', 'sugar', 'test']. Leading/trailing spaces and duplicate spaces are removed.

steps.title: Step 2 — Expand synonyms
steps.detail: Each token is looked up in a medical synonym map (synonyms.ts). 'CBC' expands to ['CBC', 'complete blood count']. 'HbA1c' expands to ['HbA1c', 'glycated haemoglobin', 'السكر التراكمي']. Every variant is searched at once so abbreviations, Arabic terms, and full names all find the same tests.

steps.title: Step 3 — SQL: LIKE + pg_trgm
steps.detail: The database runs two checks in parallel: exact substring (`LIKE '%token%'`) and trigram similarity (`word_similarity ≥ 0.3`). Trigrams split words into overlapping 3-letter chunks and score how many chunks two strings share — so 'haemoglobin' scores high against 'hemoglobin'.

steps.title: Step 4 — Levenshtein typo filter
steps.detail: SQL results are re-checked in JavaScript using edit distance. A 4+ character token allows 1 character change ('glucos' → 'glucose'); 6+ characters allows 2 changes ('haemogloin' → 'haemoglobin'). Short tokens (under 4 chars) require exact match to avoid false positives.

steps.title: Step 5 — Score and rank
steps.detail: Each result gets a relevance score combining its pg_trgm similarity and how many query tokens matched. The list is sorted best-first, so the most relevant test or lab appears at the top.

why: Real medical searches are messy: abbreviations, Arabic/English mixing, typos, and multiple names for the same test. This pipeline handles all of them without requiring the user to type perfectly.

title: The Synonym Map: One Search in Any Language
intro: The backbone of smart search is a hand-curated dictionary (synonyms.ts) that maps abbreviations, Arabic terms, and alternate names to their full equivalents — so every variation of a test name finds the same results.

cards.name: Abbreviations
cards.what: 'CBC' → 'complete blood count', 'FBS' → 'fasting blood sugar', 'TSH' → 'thyroid stimulating hormone', 'LFT' → 'liver function tests'.
cards.why: Doctors and labs abbreviate; patients search in full words. The map bridges both directions simultaneously.

cards.name: Arabic terms
cards.what: 'السكر التراكمي' → 'HbA1c', 'الغدة الدرقية' → 'thyroid', 'تعداد الدم الكامل' → 'CBC', 'وظائف الكلى' → 'kidney function'.
cards.why: Many Egyptian patients search in Arabic. The map connects Arabic disease names directly to the English test names stored in the database.

cards.name: Alternate spellings
cards.what: British and American spellings, old and new names, and common written variants are all mapped to one canonical form.
cards.why: Medical terminology evolved over decades across different countries; the same test can have several legitimate spellings.

cards.name: Panel expansion
cards.what: 'liver panel' → ['ALT', 'AST', 'bilirubin', 'albumin']. Searching a panel name returns all its component tests.
cards.why: Patients often ask for a 'kidney function check' rather than knowing the individual test names (creatinine, urea, eGFR) that make it up.

title: Code: How Fuzzy Search Works
intro: When a user types 'HBA1' instead of 'HbA1c', or 'glucos' instead of 'glucose', the search still finds the right test. It uses two techniques: Levenshtein edit distance for typos, and PostgreSQL trigrams for partial matches.
note: **Edit distance = 1** means one character changed: 'glucos' → 'glucose' (insert 'e'). **Edit distance = 2** handles bigger typos: 'haemogloin' → 'haemoglobin'. Short tokens (under 4 chars) require an exact match to avoid false positives. The database also uses **pg_trgm** (`word_similarity`) in parallel — it splits words into 3-character chunks and scores overlap. Both techniques run together so the search catches both typos and partial words.

title: Q&A: Backend Deep Questions
intro: Common questions from developers reading the code for the first time.

pairs.q: How does the search avoid SQL injection?
pairs.a: The search uses Prisma's `$queryRaw` with **tagged template literals**. Each `${value}` in the template becomes a parameterized placeholder — the database driver escapes it automatically. No string is ever concatenated into the SQL text, so there is nothing for an attacker to inject.

pairs.q: What is `word_similarity` and why not just LIKE?
pairs.a: **word_similarity** is a PostgreSQL function from the `pg_trgm` extension. It splits words into overlapping 3-character chunks (trigrams) and scores how many chunks two strings share. `LIKE '%cbc%'` only finds exact substrings. `word_similarity` also catches 'CBC' vs 'C.B.C', partial words, and minor variations. We run both in parallel: LIKE catches exact hits, pg_trgm scores fuzzy relevance.

0.3 is a balance tested against real medical terms. Lower (e.g. 0.1) returns too many irrelevant results. Higher (e.g. 0.5) misses legitimate short abbreviations. 'CBC' vs 'complete blood count' scores ~0.2 similarity (the words are totally different), so the synonym map expands the query first — **then** trigrams catch typos within each synonym.

pairs.q: How does booking prevent two people grabbing the same slot at the same moment?
pairs.a: The database has a **unique constraint** on (lab_id, appointment_time, status='Active'). Both users send their request at the same time. The backend wraps the insert in a Prisma transaction. One insert succeeds; the second violates the unique constraint and gets a database error. The backend catches that error and returns a clean '409 Slot already taken' response — no double-booking, no crash.

pairs.q: Why store only a hash of the refresh token?
pairs.a: If the database were ever breached, an attacker with plain refresh tokens could impersonate any user indefinitely. By storing only a **bcrypt/SHA-256 hash**, the attacker gets hashes they cannot reverse into real tokens. The real token lives only on the user's device and in the HTTP response — it never sits in the database in readable form.

The backend checks two things: (1) the file's **MIME type** must be `application/pdf` (not just the filename extension, which anyone can fake), and (2) the file size must be under 10 MB. The first few bytes of every PDF file start with `%PDF-` — the backend reads the buffer header to confirm this before accepting the upload.

title: How Health Trend Direction Is Computed
intro: When a patient views their glucose or HbA1c over time, a badge says 'Rising', 'Falling', or 'Stable'. That label is not guesswork — the backend computes it from the actual numbers.

steps.title: Collect readings for the chosen range
steps.detail: All comparable readings for a marker (e.g. glucose) within the selected period — 3 months, 6 months, 12 months, or all time — are fetched. Only readings already converted to the canonical unit are included.

steps.title: Check for enough data
steps.detail: If fewer than 2 readings exist, the trend is labelled 'insufficient_data' and no badge is shown. A direction needs at least two points to be meaningful.

steps.title: Calculate percent change
steps.detail: (newest − oldest) / oldest × 100. This measures how much the value has actually moved over the period.

steps.title: Apply the stability threshold
steps.detail: If the absolute change is less than 2%, the trend is 'stable' — small measurement noise doesn't count as a real change. Above 2%: positive = 'increasing', negative = 'decreasing'.

steps.title: Generate a qualitative note
steps.detail: If the latest reading is outside the reference range (e.g. glucose > 100 mg/dL fasting), the backend adds a plain-English note — 'Latest reading is above the reference range' — alongside the direction badge.

why: A raw number on a chart doesn't tell a patient if they are improving. The trend label and note turn data into a clear, actionable signal without needing a doctor to interpret it.

title: Clinical Unit Conversion: One Formula, Many Labs
intro: Lab A reports your glucose as 5.4 mmol/L. Lab B reports it as 97 mg/dL. These look completely different but mean the same thing. The backend converts both to one canonical unit so they can be plotted on the same chart.

steps.title: Match the result to a canonical marker
steps.detail: Each raw result name ('Fasting glucose', 'FBS', 'Blood Sugar') is looked up in an alias table and matched to a CanonicalMarker. This is how 'FBS' and 'Fasting glucose' are recognised as the same measurement.

steps.title: Apply the medical conversion formula
steps.detail: Each marker has a known formula: Glucose mg/dL × 0.0555 = mmol/L. HbA1c uses the NGSP formula: (% − 2.15) × 10.929 = mmol/mol. Creatinine mg/dL × 88.4 = µmol/L. Cholesterol mg/dL × 0.0259 = mmol/L.

steps.title: Mark non-convertible units
steps.detail: If the unit is not in the conversion table, the reading is stored with `is_comparable = false`. It still shows on the chart but is excluded from trend computation — a wrong conversion never reaches the patient.

steps.title: Store both original and converted values
steps.detail: The original lab value (97 mg/dL) is kept alongside the converted value (5.38 mmol/L). The patient always sees what their lab actually wrote, and a conversion note is shown if the unit was changed.

why: Without unit conversion, comparing results across different labs is impossible. This pipeline is what makes the cross-lab health trend chart medically meaningful rather than just visually pretty.

title: Lab History Privacy: Who Sees Your Past Results
intro: When a lab staff member opens a patient's booking, they can optionally see the patient's prior test history for better context. This is opt-in — the patient controls what is shared.

steps.title: Default: Same-lab-only (SAME_LAB_ONLY)
steps.detail: By default, a lab can only see the bookings and results from their own lab. A patient's history at Lab B is completely invisible to Lab A.

steps.title: Opt-in: Full history (FULL_HISTORY_AUTHORIZED)
steps.detail: The patient can choose in their profile settings to allow any approved lab to see their full cross-lab history and structured health data. Useful for patients who want their care coordinated across labs.

steps.title: PDF files stay lab-scoped — always
steps.detail: Even with full history authorized, a lab can only download PDFs from their own bookings. Structured numbers (glucose values, trend data) from other labs are shared; the original PDF documents never are.

steps.title: Safety caps on the context
steps.detail: The cross-lab context is capped at 45 bookings, 8 trend markers, and 4 data points per marker — small enough to be useful without overwhelming lab staff or the server.

why: Medical data is among the most sensitive data a person has. Sharing is opt-in, not opt-out — the patient's privacy is the default, and richer sharing is their choice to make.

title: Search Autocomplete: 3-Tier Suggestion Engine
intro: As you type in the search box, suggestions appear instantly. These are not random — each comes from one of three tiers, prioritised and blended so the most useful result appears first.

steps.title: Tier 1 — Abbreviation hints (instant, zero DB cost)
steps.detail: If you type a known abbreviation like 'CBC' or 'TSH', the first suggestion immediately expands it to the full name ('Complete Blood Count'). This is a direct lookup against the in-memory synonym map — no database query needed.

steps.title: Tier 2 — Scored test name suggestions
steps.detail: The test catalog is searched and each match is scored: prefix match = 1.0 (highest), substring match = 0.7, pg_trgm similarity = fallback score. Results rank best-first so 'Glucose' beats 'Fasting Blood Glucose Panel' for a query of 'gluco'.

steps.title: Tier 3 — Category suggestions
steps.detail: If the query matches a test category (like 'thyroid' or 'liver'), the category name is added as a suggestion. Tapping it pre-filters the entire search to that category rather than a specific test.

steps.title: Deduplicate and cap
steps.detail: Suggestions from all three tiers are merged, duplicates removed, and capped at 8 results by default (max 20). The result is a clean, ranked list with no repeated entries.

why: Search-as-you-type needs to feel instant. Resolving abbreviations in memory and using scored ranking makes the suggestion list both fast and genuinely useful for medical searches.

title: More Notable Backend Features
intro: A few more engineering decisions that make TesTly production-ready rather than just functional.

cards.name: Lab Onboarding Readiness Checker
cards.what: Before a lab goes live, the backend verifies it has: a phone number, an accreditation status, a turnaround time, at least one active test, and at least one schedule slot. Missing any item keeps the lab in PendingReview.
cards.why: A lab that skips any of these steps would confuse patients. The checker guarantees every live lab is genuinely ready to receive bookings.

cards.name: Booking Status Audit Trail
cards.what: Every change to a booking's status (Pending → Confirmed → Completed) is recorded as an immutable event row with a timestamp. The history is never edited or deleted.
cards.why: Medical and financial data must be traceable. If a dispute arises about when a result was delivered or a payment was made, the audit trail is the source of truth.

cards.name: Home Test Kit Pipeline
cards.what: Home kit bookings follow a separate status chain: AwaitingShipment → Shipped (with courier tracking number) → Delivered → SampleReceived, stored in the KitStatus enum.
cards.why: Home kits travel by courier and have different milestones than lab visits. A dedicated status chain means patients and labs always know exactly where the kit is.

cards.name: Zero-Result Logging
cards.what: Every search that returns zero results is logged with the query text and timestamp in the SearchZeroResultLog table, visible in the admin dashboard.
cards.why: If 50 patients search for 'vitamin D' and find nothing, the admin sees it. Zero-result logs turn failed searches into actionable catalog improvement signals.

title: Putting It All Together
subtitle: One brain, many safe jobs
intro: The backend is the trusted middle layer that makes TesTly work. Everything a patient or lab does passes through it, gets checked, and is stored correctly.
bullets: One backend serves both the website and the phone app.
Layered safety: login check, role check, lab-approval check, and ownership check.
Smart algorithms make results comparable, bookings race-proof, and logins secure.
Built to handle real money and real medical data carefully.
